questions.explanation: cloudbuild.yaml supports a top-level artifacts section where you declare objects with a location (gs:// bucket path) and paths (glob). Cloud Build uploads matching files after the build completes, and they appear in the build's artifact manifest. Using gsutil cp in a step works but bypasses the native artifact tracking that makes files linkable from the build record. logsBucket controls only build logs, not artifacts.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your organization mandates that all Cloud Build steps use only approved base images stored internally, and no step may pull from Docker Hub. You also need to set a global environment variable ENVIRONMENT=production visible to every step without repeating it. Which pair of cloudbuild.yaml features satisfies both requirements?
options: Set the name field of each step to your Artifact Registry path, and use the top-level options.env list to declare global environment variables
Use the images field at the top level to whitelist Docker Hub images, and set environment variables in each step individually
Set the logsBucket to block external image pulls, and use substitutions instead of environment variables
Configure a build trigger filter that rejects steps with Docker Hub image references, and use a secret manager secret for the environment value
questions.explanation: In cloudbuild.yaml, the name field of each step specifies the container image to run; pointing it to your Artifact Registry ensures no Docker Hub images are pulled. The top-level options.env list defines environment variables that are injected into every step, avoiding repetition. The images field is for publishing final images, not whitelisting sources. Substitutions are for parameterizing values, not for injecting runtime environment variables globally.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your team is migrating container images from Container Registry to Artifact Registry. Which statement correctly describes a key operational difference between the two services?
options: Artifact Registry supports only Docker images; Container Registry supports Helm charts and npm packages
Artifact Registry supports multiple format repositories (Docker, Maven, npm, Python, etc.) within a single project and region, while Container Registry supports only Docker images
Container Registry is the newer service and is preferred for all new workloads on Google Cloud
Artifact Registry and Container Registry share the same storage bucket; they differ only in the API endpoint
questions.explanation: Artifact Registry is the successor to Container Registry and supports multiple artifact formats including Docker images, Maven, npm, Python, Go, and Helm charts in regionally-scoped repositories. Container Registry supports only Docker images and is in maintenance mode. They use separate storage backends; Container Registry stores images in Cloud Storage buckets while Artifact Registry uses its own managed storage.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: A security auditor requires that every container image stored in Artifact Registry be automatically scanned for known OS and language-package vulnerabilities, with no manual step. How do you enable this?
options: Enable the Container Analysis API and turn on automatic scanning in the Artifact Registry repository settings; scans run on every push
Schedule a daily Cloud Build job that runs trivy against each image in the repository
Configure a Binary Authorization policy that blocks deployment but does not scan images
Enable VPC Service Controls on the project; this implicitly activates vulnerability scanning
questions.explanation: Artifact Registry integrates with Container Analysis to provide on-push vulnerability scanning. Once the Container Analysis API is enabled and scanning is turned on for the repository, every newly pushed image is scanned automatically and findings appear as Occurrences in the Container Analysis API. Binary Authorization enforces attestations at deploy time but does not scan images. VPC Service Controls restrict API access perimeters and have no effect on vulnerability scanning.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: You are setting up access control for an Artifact Registry Docker repository. A Cloud Build service account in project A must be able to push images, and a GKE node service account in project B must be able to pull images. Which TWO IAM bindings are required? (Choose TWO)
options: Grant the Cloud Build service account from project A the roles/artifactregistry.writer role on the Artifact Registry repository in project A
Grant the GKE node service account from project B the roles/artifactregistry.reader role on the Artifact Registry repository in project A
Grant the Cloud Build service account from project A the roles/storage.objectCreator role on the GCS bucket backing Artifact Registry
Grant the GKE node service account from project B the roles/container.developer role in project B
questions.explanation: roles/artifactregistry.writer grants push (write) access to a repository and is the correct role for the Cloud Build service account that publishes images. roles/artifactregistry.reader grants pull (read) access and is the correct role for the GKE node service account in the consuming project. Artifact Registry does not use GCS buckets, so storage roles are irrelevant. roles/container.developer is a GKE cluster administration role unrelated to image registry access.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: A financial services company requires that all container images deployed to production carry a cryptographic attestation confirming they passed a security scan and were built by an approved Cloud Build pipeline. After pushing a signed image to Artifact Registry, what GCP mechanism enforces that only attested images are deployed to GKE?
options: Artifact Registry tag immutability, which prevents image tags from being overwritten
Binary Authorization, which evaluates attestations at pod admission time and blocks any image that lacks a valid attestation from a trusted attestor
Cloud Armor policies applied to the GKE ingress, which inspect image metadata before allowing traffic
Container Analysis Occurrences, which automatically block deployment if a CRITICAL vulnerability is found
questions.explanation: Binary Authorization is Google Cloud's deploy-time admission controller for GKE. It evaluates attestations stored in Container Analysis against a policy and rejects pods that reference images without valid attestations signed by a configured trusted attestor. Tag immutability prevents tag mutation but does not enforce attestations. Cloud Armor operates at the network/HTTP layer and has no visibility into image metadata. Container Analysis records findings but does not directly block Kubernetes deployments.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your organization runs Cloud Build pipelines that must reach internal services hosted on a VPC, including a private Artifact Registry mirror and an on-premises test database connected via Cloud VPN. The default public Cloud Build pool cannot reach these resources. What is the correct solution?
options: Add firewall rules to allow Cloud Build's public IP ranges into the VPC
Create a Cloud Build private pool peered to the VPC that hosts the private resources; build jobs running on the private pool can reach VPC-internal addresses
Enable Private Google Access on the VPC subnet; this allows Cloud Build to connect to VPC resources
Deploy a proxy VM in the VPC and route all build traffic through it using cloudbuild.yaml step environment variables
questions.explanation: Cloud Build private pools are dedicated worker pools that can be VPC-peered to your network, giving build steps direct access to RFC 1918 addresses inside the VPC including private Artifact Registry mirrors, Cloud SQL instances, and on-premises resources reached via VPN or Interconnect. The default pool shares public infrastructure and cannot reach private VPC resources. Private Google Access enables VMs without external IPs to reach Google APIs, not the reverse situation. A proxy VM introduces operational overhead and is not the recommended pattern.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your security team requires that Cloud Build workers for regulated workloads never have internet egress -- all traffic must stay within Google's network or route to approved internal endpoints. You have provisioned a private pool. Which configuration enforces no-internet-egress on that pool?
options: Set the privatePoolV1Config.networkConfig.egressOption to NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS when creating or updating the private pool
Apply a VPC firewall rule with deny-all egress on the VPC; private pool workers automatically inherit it
Enable VPC Service Controls on the project; this blocks all internet egress from compute resources including Cloud Build
Set the cloudbuild.yaml top-level option timeout to a low value to limit outbound connection attempts
questions.explanation: Cloud Build private pools expose a networkConfig.egressOption field. Setting it to NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS configures the worker pool so that all traffic is routed through the peered VPC and no direct internet egress is permitted. VPC firewall deny-all egress rules apply to VM instances in the VPC but private pool workers reside in a Google-managed tenant project and are not directly governed by customer firewall rules unless traffic routes through the peered network. VPC Service Controls restricts API access at the project boundary but does not block internet egress from compute workers.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: A team provisions a Cloud Build private pool to build container images that are pushed to a private Artifact Registry repository in the same VPC. After setup, builds fail because the workers cannot resolve the Artifact Registry hostname. What is the most likely cause and fix?
options: The private pool's machine type is too small to run DNS resolution; upgrade to a larger machine type

Private Google Access is not enabled on the VPC subnet used for private pool peering, so the workers cannot resolve and reach Google API endpoints over internal addresses; enable Private Google Access on that subnet
The Artifact Registry repository is in the wrong region; move it to match the private pool region
Cloud Build private pools do not support Artifact Registry; use Container Registry instead
questions.explanation: When a Cloud Build private pool is peered to a VPC, the workers use the customer VPC's DNS and routing. Private Google Access must be enabled on the subnet so that instances (and peered workers) can reach Google APIs such as Artifact Registry using internal IP addresses without traversing the internet. Without Private Google Access, DNS resolution for *.pkg.dev fails or routes incorrectly and push/pull operations time out. Machine type and region do not affect DNS resolution. Artifact Registry is fully supported with private pools.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your team is adopting Google Cloud Deploy to manage progressive delivery of a containerized service to three environments: dev, staging, and production. You need to ensure that a release cannot proceed to production without an authorized manager explicitly approving the rollout. Which Cloud Deploy feature should you configure?
options: Set a deploy timeout on the production target so the rollout pauses and waits for manual intervention
Enable requireApproval: true on the production target definition in the delivery pipeline; a rollout to that target will wait in PENDING_APPROVAL state until approved via the console or gcloud
Create a Cloud Build approval step that runs a gcloud deploy rollouts approve command before promoting
Configure an Automation resource with the repair rollout action on the production target
questions.explanation: Cloud Deploy targets support a requireApproval field. When set to true, any rollout advancing to that target enters PENDING_APPROVAL state and cannot proceed until an authorized user with the clouddeploy.approver role approves it via the console, gcloud, or the API. Deploy timeouts affect how long a rollout attempt runs, not whether it starts. A Cloud Build step calling gcloud approve would require the pipeline to already be in progress and creates a circular dependency. Automation resources handle repair and promotion logic but do not implement human approval gates.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: A release was successfully deployed to the staging target in a Cloud Deploy delivery pipeline. You now want to advance it to the production target. Which command promotes the release to the next target in the pipeline sequence?
options: gcloud deploy releases create --delivery-pipeline=my-pipeline --to-target=production
gcloud deploy rollouts create --release=my-release --delivery-pipeline=my-pipeline --to-target=production
gcloud deploy releases promote --release=my-release --delivery-pipeline=my-pipeline --to-target=production
gcloud deploy targets advance --target=production --delivery-pipeline=my-pipeline --release=my-release
questions.explanation: gcloud deploy releases promote advances an existing release from its current stage to the next target in the pipeline, optionally overriding the target with --to-target. A release is created once and then promoted through targets; gcloud deploy releases create would generate a new release rather than advancing the existing one. gcloud deploy rollouts create is not a valid command for promotion. There is no gcloud deploy targets advance subcommand.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your organization uses Cloud Deploy with a delivery pipeline that has three targets: dev, staging, and production. After a bad production deploy you need to immediately roll back to the previously deployed release version on the production target. What is the correct approach?
options: Delete the current rollout resource; Cloud Deploy will automatically redeploy the previous release
Use gcloud deploy releases promote to advance the previous release from staging to production again
Use gcloud deploy rollouts rollback to create a new rollout on the production target that redeploys the last successful release
Manually apply the previous Kubernetes manifests with kubectl apply to bypass Cloud Deploy
questions.explanation: Cloud Deploy provides a built-in rollback capability via gcloud deploy rollouts rollback, which creates a new rollout on the specified target that redeploys the previously successful release. This keeps the full audit trail intact and respects approval gates. Deleting a rollout resource does not trigger redeployment. Re-promoting from staging is slower and may deploy a different version than what was last successful in production. Applying manifests directly with kubectl bypasses Cloud Deploy's tracking and approval mechanisms.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: You are modeling a Cloud Deploy delivery pipeline that must deploy a service to five regional GKE clusters simultaneously, rather than sequentially. How should you configure the pipeline to achieve parallel deployment to all five regions at once?
options: List all five cluster targets in a single stages entry using the targetId field; Cloud Deploy deploys them in parallel
Create a parallel deployment stage using the parallelDeployment block and list all five targets within it
Define a single stage that references a multi-target containing all five regional cluster targets; Cloud Deploy rolls out to all child targets in parallel
Create five separate delivery pipelines, one per region, and invoke them simultaneously from Cloud Build
questions.explanation: Cloud Deploy supports multi-targets, which are logical groupings of multiple physical targets. When a pipeline stage references a multi-target, Cloud Deploy creates a child rollout for each physical target and runs them in parallel. Listing targets directly in a stages entry is not valid syntax. There is no parallelDeployment block in Cloud Deploy's schema. Using five separate pipelines creates tracking overhead and loses the unified release model.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: A Cloud Deploy pipeline deploys to a GKE target. You want Cloud Deploy to automatically verify the deployment succeeded by running a custom verification job after each rollout, and to mark the rollout FAILED if the verification exits non-zero. Which Cloud Deploy feature enables this?
options: Configure a postdeploy action in the pipeline stage that runs a Cloud Build trigger after each rollout
Define a verify block in the deployParameters of the target with a container image that runs the health check
Enable verify: true in the delivery pipeline stage and provide a skaffold verify configuration with the test container commands
Create a Cloud Monitoring alerting policy that transitions the rollout state to FAILED when error rate exceeds a threshold
questions.explanation: Cloud Deploy integrates with Skaffold's verify feature. When verify: true is set in a pipeline stage, Cloud Deploy runs the skaffold verify phase after deployment, which executes user-defined test containers or commands. If any verify job exits non-zero, Cloud Deploy marks the rollout as FAILED and stops further promotion. A postdeploy Cloud Build trigger is an external mechanism that does not feed back into rollout state. The deployParameters block configures runtime values for manifests, not verification. Cloud Monitoring alerting policies cannot directly change rollout state.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: You are deploying a new version of a stateless API service on Cloud Run. You want to shift 10% of traffic to the new revision immediately, monitor error rates for 30 minutes, and only then route all traffic to the new revision. Which deployment strategy should you use?
options: Blue/green deployment: deploy the new revision with 100% traffic, then switch the blue environment to green after validation
Canary deployment: deploy the new revision with 10% traffic weight using a Cloud Run traffic split, then gradually increase to 100% after validation
Rolling update: configure maxSurge and maxUnavailable on the Cloud Run service to gradually replace instances
In-place deployment: update the existing revision's container image URL without changing traffic routing
questions.explanation: A canary deployment routes a small percentage of real traffic to the new version so you can validate behavior with limited blast radius before full rollout. Cloud Run natively supports traffic splits by revision, making this straightforward to implement. Blue/green swaps 100% of traffic at once, which does not match the incremental validation requirement. Cloud Run revisions are immutable and do not use maxSurge/maxUnavailable rolling parameters (those are Kubernetes constructs). In-place image update would create a new revision but without controlled traffic splitting.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your team runs a GKE workload and wants to implement a blue/green deployment so that you can instantly switch all traffic back to the previous version if issues arise after a release. Which TWO Kubernetes resources are central to implementing blue/green deployments on GKE?
options: A Kubernetes Service whose selector is updated to point to the green Deployment once validation passes
A HorizontalPodAutoscaler that scales down the blue Deployment after traffic shifts to green
Two separate Deployments (blue and green) running concurrently with distinct label selectors
A PodDisruptionBudget on the blue Deployment to prevent it from being deleted during the switch
questions.explanation: Blue/green on GKE relies on two parallel Deployments (blue = current, green = new) each with distinct pod labels. A single Kubernetes Service acts as the traffic entry point; its label selector is updated atomically to point to the green Deployment when you are ready to cut over, and reverted to blue for instant rollback. HorizontalPodAutoscaler and PodDisruptionBudget are supporting resources that may be used but are not the core mechanism of a blue/green switch.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: You are deploying a GKE Deployment with 20 replicas. You configure a rolling update strategy with maxSurge: 2 and maxUnavailable: 0. What is the effect of these settings during an update?
options: Up to 2 old pods may be terminated before new pods are ready, but no extra pods beyond 20 are created
At most 2 new pods are created at a time; no old pods are terminated until the new pods pass readiness checks; total pod count may temporarily reach 22
All 20 pods are replaced simultaneously, then 2 new pods are added to reach 22 total
maxUnavailable: 0 causes the update to fail because there must always be at least one unavailable slot for new pods
questions.explanation: With maxUnavailable: 0, Kubernetes will not terminate any old pods until replacement pods are ready, ensuring zero availability loss. maxSurge: 2 allows up to 2 extra pods above the desired count to be created at a time during the update, so the cluster temporarily runs up to 22 pods while each batch of 2 is replaced. This is a safe, zero-downtime rolling update at the cost of briefly requiring extra node capacity. Option A reverses the roles of the two parameters. Options C and D are incorrect descriptions of rolling update behavior.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your team deploys an App Engine Standard service. You want to implement a canary release by sending 5% of traffic to a new version while keeping 95% on the current version, with the ability to roll back instantly. Which App Engine feature allows this?
options: Deploy the new version and use traffic splitting to assign 5% of traffic to it by version ID; revert by re-splitting all traffic to the old version
Use App Engine flexible environment, which supports Kubernetes-style rolling updates with traffic percentages
Set the new version as the default with 5% serving allocation; App Engine will automatically increase it based on error rates
Deploy the new version to a separate App Engine service and use Cloud Load Balancing to split traffic 95/5 between services
questions.explanation: App Engine Standard supports traffic splitting at the version level, allowing you to allocate specific percentages of incoming requests to different deployed versions of the same service. Setting 5% to the new version and 95% to the existing version is exactly a canary pattern. Rollback is instant by re-assigning 100% traffic to the old version. App Engine Standard and Flexible are separate environments with different capabilities; Standard directly supports version-level traffic splitting. App Engine does not auto-scale traffic based on error rates. Using a separate service and load balancer is unnecessary complexity.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: You are using Cloud Deploy to deploy a GKE service with a canary strategy. The pipeline is configured with a canaryDeployment block specifying percentages [25, 50, 75, 100]. During the 50% phase, your monitoring dashboard shows the new version's error rate has spiked to 8%. What is the BEST action to take using Cloud Deploy?
options: Manually edit the Kubernetes Deployment resource to set replicas for the canary to zero
Use gcloud deploy rollouts rollback to revert the rollout on that target, which will restore the previous stable version
Wait for the 75% phase to begin, then initiate rollback from the console
Delete the Cloud Deploy release and redeploy the previous release from source control
questions.explanation: When a canary rollout is in progress and metrics indicate a problem, the correct Cloud Deploy action is gcloud deploy rollouts rollback, which creates a new rollout targeting the last known good release on that target. This cleanly reverses the canary and restores stable traffic. Manually editing Kubernetes resources bypasses Cloud Deploy's tracking and can leave the pipeline in an inconsistent state. Waiting for the 75% phase would expose more users to the failing version. Deleting the release loses audit history and is more disruptive than a targeted rollback.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your team needs to choose a deployment strategy for a GKE service that: (1) produces zero downtime during deploys, (2) requires no extra cluster capacity beyond the normal replica count, and (3) allows instant rollback by restoring the previous Deployment spec. Which TWO statements correctly characterize a blue/green strategy versus a rolling update strategy in this context?
options: Blue/green requires running two full Deployments simultaneously and therefore needs extra cluster capacity; rolling updates can achieve zero downtime with maxUnavailable: 0 but require only maxSurge extra capacity
Rolling updates do not support instant rollback; once pods are replaced they cannot be restored without redeploying
Blue/green enables instant rollback by flipping the Service selector back to the blue Deployment, with no pod restart required
Rolling updates with maxUnavailable: 0 and maxSurge: 0 produce zero downtime with no extra capacity required
questions.explanation: Blue/green keeps both the old (blue) and new (green) Deployments running simultaneously, which requires roughly double the pod capacity during the transition window. However, rollback is instant -- simply flip the Service selector back to the blue Deployment without restarting any pods. Rolling updates with maxSurge can achieve near-zero downtime but do not provide the same instant rollback path; Kubernetes rolling update history does allow kubectl rollout undo, but pods must restart. Setting both maxSurge and maxUnavailable to 0 is invalid and will be rejected by the API.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your Cloud Build pipeline builds a Node.js application. Each build downloads hundreds of npm packages and takes 8 minutes, but the package.json rarely changes. You want to dramatically reduce build time using caching. Which approach is MOST effective for this workload?
options: Increase the Cloud Build machine type to n1-highcpu-32 so npm install completes faster
Store the node_modules directory in a Cloud Storage cache bucket and restore it at the start of each build using gsutil; skip npm install if package-lock.json is unchanged
Use kaniko with --cache=true so that each npm install layer is pushed to Artifact Registry and reused on subsequent builds
Split the build into two Cloud Build triggers: one for dependency installation and one for the application build
questions.explanation: For dependency-heavy workloads like Node.js, caching the node_modules directory in a Cloud Storage bucket and restoring it when package-lock.json has not changed eliminates redundant npm installs. Cloud Build supports this pattern natively via the volumes and artifacts.objects configuration or with explicit gsutil cp steps. Kaniko layer caching is effective for Docker image layer reuse but does not help with npm dependency resolution outside of a Dockerfile RUN layer. Increasing machine type speeds up compute but does not reduce network-bound dependency downloads. Splitting into two triggers adds complexity without solving the core caching problem.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your team builds Docker container images in Cloud Build using kaniko instead of the standard Docker daemon. You want to enable layer caching so that unchanged Dockerfile layers are reused across builds. Which TWO arguments must you pass to the kaniko executor to enable remote layer caching backed by Artifact Registry?
options: --cache=true to enable layer caching
--cache-repo=REGION-docker.pkg.dev/PROJECT/REPO to specify the Artifact Registry repository used to store and retrieve cached layers
--snapshot-mode=redo to force layer re-evaluation even when cache hits occur
--no-push to prevent the final image from being pushed after a cached build
questions.explanation: Kaniko's remote layer caching requires two flags: --cache=true to activate caching behavior, and --cache-repo pointing to an OCI-compatible registry such as Artifact Registry where cached layers are stored and retrieved. Together they allow kaniko to push intermediate layers to the cache repo and pull them on subsequent builds when Dockerfile instructions have not changed, substantially reducing build times. --snapshot-mode=redo disables caching effectiveness by forcing re-snapshots. --no-push prevents the final image from being pushed, which is the opposite of what a caching build pipeline needs.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your CI/CD pipeline runs unit tests, integration tests, and a smoke test suite in Cloud Build. Integration tests require a live database. The pipeline is slow because integration tests run on every commit, even when only frontend TypeScript files changed. What is the BEST strategy to reduce pipeline duration while maintaining safety?
options: Remove integration tests from the pipeline and run them manually before each release
Run integration tests only in a separate nightly pipeline and remove them from the commit-triggered pipeline entirely
Use Cloud Build's includedFiles filter on the integration test trigger so it only fires when backend source files or schema files change; keep unit tests and smoke tests on every commit
Parallelize all three test stages by running them in separate Cloud Build steps with no waitFor dependencies
questions.explanation: Cloud Build trigger configuration supports includedFiles and ignoredFiles glob patterns. By restricting the integration test trigger to backend source files and database schema files, you avoid running expensive database-backed tests when only frontend code changes, directly reducing pipeline duration. Unit tests and smoke tests remain on every commit for fast feedback. Removing integration tests entirely from the commit pipeline increases the risk of merging broken backend changes. Running integration tests only nightly delays detection of regressions. Parallelizing tests helps with wall-clock time but does not eliminate redundant test runs triggered by irrelevant file changes.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your team wants to add a load test stage to the CI/CD pipeline that runs against a staging environment after every successful deployment. The load test should block promotion to production if the p99 latency exceeds 500ms under a 200 requests-per-second load. Which combination of tools and pipeline actions BEST implements this requirement?
options: Run a Cloud Scheduler job after each deployment to trigger a load test Cloud Run job; export results to BigQuery and check them manually before approving promotion

Add a Cloud Build step that runs a load testing tool (such as k6 or Locust) against the staging URL, parses the p99 latency from the output, and fails the step with a non-zero exit code if the threshold is exceeded; configure the Cloud Deploy pipeline to run this as a postdeploy action that blocks promotion on failure
Configure a Cloud Monitoring SLO with a p99 latency objective and rely on the SLO burn rate alert to notify engineers to stop promotion manually
Run the load test as a Cloud Build step with waitFor: ['-'] so it executes in parallel with the deployment and does not block promotion
questions.explanation: The correct approach embeds the load test as a pipeline step that produces a deterministic pass/fail signal. Running a load test tool (k6, Locust, or similar) in Cloud Build, checking p99 latency programmatically, and exiting non-zero on threshold breach gives the pipeline a hard gate. When configured as a Cloud Deploy postdeploy action, a non-zero exit fails the rollout verification and blocks automatic promotion to production. Cloud Scheduler introduces asynchrony and manual review, which does not provide an automated gate. An SLO burn rate alert is reactive and requires manual intervention rather than automated blocking. Using waitFor: ['-'] would run the test in parallel with deployment, potentially checking latency before the deployment is stable and not blocking promotion.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your organization requires that every container image deployed to production must have been built by a Cloud Build trigger, not pushed manually by a developer. Which GCP mechanism enforces this at deploy time?
options: IAM policy that denies the Artifact Registry writer role to all human user accounts
Binary Authorization policy requiring an attestation that is only created by the Cloud Build pipeline's service account after a successful build
VPC Service Controls perimeter that blocks Artifact Registry pushes from outside the corporate network
Artifact Registry tag immutability policy that prevents overwriting the production tag
questions.explanation: Binary Authorization enforces deployment policies by requiring cryptographic attestations on images before they can be deployed to GKE or Cloud Run. By configuring the pipeline's Cloud Build service account as the only attestor, and requiring that attestation on the production policy, images pushed manually without going through the pipeline will lack the attestation and be blocked at deploy time. IAM restrictions on push rights help but do not prevent an authorized CI service account from being misused. VPC Service Controls restrict API access perimeters, not pipeline provenance. Tag immutability prevents overwriting but does not enforce build provenance.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: A Cloud Build trigger is configured to fire on pushes to any branch matching the pattern ^feature/.*$. A developer pushes to feature/new-login. The build fails at the unit test step. What happens to subsequent steps in the cloudbuild.yaml?
options: All remaining steps are skipped because a non-zero exit from any step halts the build by default
Remaining steps run because Cloud Build continues after failures unless the failFast option is set
The build retries the failed step three times before proceeding to the next step
Only steps that explicitly declare waitFor on the failed step are skipped; independent steps still run
questions.explanation: In Cloud Build, if a step exits with a non-zero status code, the entire build is immediately marked as failed and no subsequent steps execute. This is the default behavior and does not require any special configuration. There is no automatic retry mechanism for failed steps. Steps that do not declare a waitFor dependency on the failed step are still skipped because the build-level failure terminates all queued work.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your team wants to use Cloud Build to build and push a multi-architecture container image (linux/amd64 and linux/arm64) to Artifact Registry in a single pipeline run. Which approach is correct for Cloud Build?
options: Run two separate Cloud Build triggers in parallel, one per architecture, and merge the manifests with a third trigger
Use a Cloud Build step that invokes docker buildx with the --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 flag and pushes a multi-arch manifest to Artifact Registry
Cloud Build does not support multi-architecture builds; use Cloud Run jobs with separate ARM and AMD workers
Use the built-in Cloud Build machine type arm-e2-medium to produce ARM images automatically alongside the default AMD build
questions.explanation: Docker Buildx with the --platform flag can produce multi-architecture images and push a single manifest list to any OCI-compatible registry including Artifact Registry. This can be done in a single Cloud Build step using the docker:latest or a custom buildx-enabled image. Splitting into two triggers adds orchestration complexity. Cloud Build does support multi-arch builds via Buildx. The arm-e2-medium machine type builds on ARM hardware but does not automatically produce a multi-arch manifest.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: You need to pass a database password from Secret Manager to a Cloud Build step without the value appearing in the build logs or the cloudbuild.yaml file. Which Cloud Build feature handles this securely?
options: Store the password as a Cloud Build substitution variable prefixed with _; Cloud Build automatically redacts substitution values from logs
Use the availableSecrets block in cloudbuild.yaml to reference the Secret Manager secret; the value is injected as an environment variable and automatically redacted in build logs
Base64-encode the password and store it in a Cloud Storage bucket; have the step decode it at runtime
Store the password as a Cloud Build trigger environment variable; trigger variables are never written to logs
questions.explanation: Cloud Build's availableSecrets block integrates directly with Secret Manager. You specify the secret resource name and map it to an environment variable name; Cloud Build fetches the secret version at build time, injects it into the step, and automatically redacts occurrences of the value in build logs. Substitution variables are not redacted in logs by default. Base64 encoding is not encryption and the decoded value would appear in logs if echoed. Trigger environment variables are not natively redacted from step logs.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your Cloud Build pipeline builds a Java Maven project. The Maven dependency download phase accounts for 6 of 10 total build minutes, but the pom.xml changes infrequently. You want to cache the Maven local repository between builds. Which two configuration elements in cloudbuild.yaml enable this? (Choose TWO)
options: Declare a named volume (e.g., m2-cache) in the volumes section of the step that mounts at /root/.m2; volumes persist within a build but not across builds

Use the artifacts.objects section to upload the /root/.m2 directory to a Cloud Storage bucket at the end of each build, and add an initial step that restores it with gsutil rsync at the start of each build
Set options.volumes at the top level to declare a persistent volume backed by a Cloud Storage bucket that Cloud Build mounts automatically
Add a cacheFrom directive in the Maven step pointing to the Artifact Registry Maven repository so dependencies are fetched from there
questions.explanation: Cross-build caching for Maven requires explicitly saving and restoring the local repository. The correct pattern is: (1) at build start, use gsutil rsync to restore ~/.m2 from a Cloud Storage bucket; (2) at build end, use artifacts.objects or gsutil rsync to persist the updated cache back. Additionally, configuring Maven to use Artifact Registry as a remote repository (via settings.xml and a cacheFrom-equivalent mirror) means dependencies are fetched from the internal registry rather than Maven Central, reducing latency on cache misses. Named volumes in Cloud Build persist only within a single build run, not across builds. Cloud Build does not support persistent volumes backed by Cloud Storage at the options level.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: A delivery pipeline in Cloud Deploy has three stages: dev, staging, production. A release was promoted to staging successfully. Now you want to promote it to production but override one of the Kubernetes manifest values -- specifically the replica count -- without modifying the source manifests. Which Cloud Deploy feature allows this?
options: Edit the release's Kubernetes manifests directly in the Cloud Deploy release artifact store before promoting
Use deployParameters in the production target definition to specify runtime parameter overrides that Skaffold applies when rendering manifests for that target
Create a new release with a modified clouddeploy.yaml that has the updated replica count hardcoded
Use a Cloud Build substitution to override the replica count; pass it as a trigger parameter at promotion time
questions.explanation: Cloud Deploy supports deployParameters at the target level, which are key-value pairs passed to Skaffold at render time and used to override values in Helm charts, Kustomize overlays, or Skaffold profiles. This allows production-specific values (like increased replica counts) to be defined in the target configuration rather than in the release manifests, enabling the same release to be deployed with environment-appropriate settings. Editing release artifacts directly is not supported and would break provenance. Creating a new release for a config-only change is wasteful and loses the promotion chain. Cloud Build substitutions apply at build time, not at Cloud Deploy promotion time.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your organization has multiple teams each with their own Cloud Build triggers and pipelines. The security team wants to enforce that all builds in the organization use only a pre-approved list of base images for the first step. Which approach enforces this at scale without requiring each team to self-enforce?
options: Send all teams an email with the approved image list and rely on code review to catch violations
Create an Organization Policy constraint that restricts Cloud Build steps to approved container images
Use Binary Authorization to attest each approved base image and configure a Cloud Build build-level policy that rejects builds starting with unapproved images
Configure a Cloud Build trigger filter on each team's project that inspects the cloudbuild.yaml and fails the build if unapproved images are detected
questions.explanation: While Organization Policy constraints cover many GCP resources, Cloud Build does not expose a native org policy constraint for restricting step images. The practical enforcement pattern is a pipeline-level gate: a validation step at the start of each build that inspects the build configuration and fails if unapproved images are referenced. This can be centrally deployed via a shared Cloud Build trigger template or enforced via a pre-build hook. Binary Authorization operates at deploy time and checks deployed container images, not Cloud Build step images. Email-based self-enforcement is not a technical control.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: You are configuring a Cloud Deploy delivery pipeline for a Cloud Run service. After each successful deployment to the staging target, you want Cloud Deploy to automatically promote the release to production without human intervention. Which Cloud Deploy feature automates this?
options: Set autoPromote: true in the staging stage of the delivery pipeline configuration
Create a Cloud Deploy Automation resource with the promote action triggered on a successful rollout to the staging target
Configure a Cloud Build trigger that runs gcloud deploy releases promote after each successful staging deploy
Enable continuous delivery mode on the delivery pipeline; Cloud Deploy promotes automatically when all post-deploy verifications pass
questions.explanation: Cloud Deploy Automation resources define automated actions that trigger based on pipeline events. An Automation with the promote action and a trigger condition of successful rollout completion on the staging target will automatically create a promotion to the next target in the pipeline sequence. This is the native Cloud Deploy mechanism for continuous delivery workflows. There is no autoPromote field in the pipeline stage configuration. A Cloud Build trigger that calls gcloud is an external workaround that loses the unified pipeline model. There is no 'continuous delivery mode' setting on a delivery pipeline.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: A team pushes a Cloud Run service update. The new revision is deployed but the team wants to validate it serves traffic correctly before it receives any user traffic. Which Cloud Run deployment pattern supports deploying a new revision with zero traffic, testing it directly, and then migrating traffic?
options: Deploy the new revision using --no-traffic flag so it receives 0% of traffic; invoke it directly via its revision URL for validation; then update traffic splits to route users to it
Deploy the new revision normally; Cloud Run always holds traffic at 0% until a health check passes
Use Cloud Deploy with a canary strategy starting at 0% and requiring manual approval before any traffic is assigned
Deploy to a separate Cloud Run service, run validation, then update Cloud Load Balancing to point to the new service
questions.explanation: Cloud Run supports the --no-traffic flag on gcloud run deploy, which deploys the new revision without shifting any user traffic to it. Each revision in Cloud Run has a unique URL that can be used to send direct test traffic regardless of the service's traffic split. After validation, you update the traffic split with gcloud run services update-traffic to route user traffic to the validated revision. Cloud Run does not hold traffic at 0% pending health checks; it assigns traffic based on the configured split. Cloud Deploy canary at 0% does not expose a revision URL for direct testing without traffic configuration.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your team's Cloud Build pipeline produces a Docker image and pushes it to Artifact Registry. You want to enforce that the image tag used in production Kubernetes manifests always matches a specific immutable digest, not a mutable tag like 'latest'. Which practice enforces this?
options: Configure Artifact Registry tag immutability so the 'latest' tag cannot be overwritten

In the Cloud Build step, after pushing the image, use docker inspect or gcloud artifacts docker images describe to retrieve the SHA256 digest; write the digest reference (image@sha256:...) into the Kubernetes manifest before deploying
Use the $SHORT_SHA Cloud Build substitution as the image tag; short SHAs are immutable by definition
Enable Binary Authorization in dry-run mode; it logs digest mismatches without blocking deployments
questions.explanation: Image digests (SHA256) are content-addressed and immutable: a given digest always refers to the exact same image layers. By resolving the pushed image's digest and writing it into the manifest as image@sha256:..., you guarantee the deployed artifact is exactly what was built and tested, immune to tag mutation. Tag immutability on Artifact Registry prevents overwriting a tag but the tag itself still refers to a mutable pointer concept across different pushes. $SHORT_SHA is a useful tag for traceability but is still a tag, not a digest reference. Binary Authorization dry-run mode does not block deployments.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: A Cloud Build pipeline deploys infrastructure using Terraform. The pipeline must: (1) initialize Terraform, (2) run terraform plan and save the output, (3) require a human to review the plan before apply. Which design correctly introduces a human gate between plan and apply?
options: Add a sleep 3600 step between plan and apply to give engineers time to review

Split into two Cloud Build triggers: a plan trigger that saves the plan file to Cloud Storage and posts the plan output to a review channel; an apply trigger that is manually invoked after review, downloads the saved plan, and runs terraform apply
Use a single trigger with a long timeout; the apply step polls a Cloud Storage flag file set by an engineer
Use Cloud Deploy with requireApproval on the production target to pause between plan and apply
questions.explanation: The correct pattern separates plan and apply into two distinct Cloud Build triggers. The plan trigger runs terraform plan -out=tfplan, saves the binary plan file to Cloud Storage with a build ID in the path, and optionally posts the human-readable plan to Slack or an issue tracker. The apply trigger is manually invoked, downloads the saved plan file, and runs terraform apply tfplan -- ensuring apply executes exactly the reviewed plan. A sleep step is unreliable and blocks the worker. Polling a flag file is a fragile anti-pattern. Cloud Deploy manages container deployments and does not integrate with Terraform plan/apply workflows.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your team uses Artifact Registry to store both Docker images and npm packages. A developer reports that npm install is failing in CI with a 403 error when trying to fetch packages from the Artifact Registry npm repository. The Cloud Build service account has roles/artifactregistry.reader on the repository. What is the most likely additional configuration step that is missing?
options: Grant the Cloud Build service account roles/artifactregistry.writer instead of reader; writer includes read permissions

Configure an .npmrc file in the build step that sets the registry URL and uses the gcloud credential helper or an access token for authentication, since npm does not natively use GCP service account credentials
Enable Private Google Access on the VPC subnet used by the Cloud Build private pool
Add the npm registry URL to the Artifact Registry repository's allowed origins list
questions.explanation: Artifact Registry npm repositories require that the npm client be configured to use the correct registry URL and an authentication token. npm does not automatically use GCP Application Default Credentials. The standard approach is to generate a token using gcloud auth print-access-token or use the Artifact Registry credential helper (npx google-artifactregistry-auth) to populate the .npmrc file with the correct auth token before running npm install. The IAM role grants the GCP-level permission but does not configure npm's authentication mechanism. Writer includes reader permissions but does not resolve the npm authentication gap. Private Google Access is a network-level configuration unrelated to npm auth.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: You are setting up a Cloud Build trigger that must only fire when both of these conditions are true: the push is to the main branch AND the changed files include at least one file under the src/ directory. Which trigger configuration achieves this?
options: Set branchName: main and add src/** to the includedFiles filter in the trigger configuration
Create two separate triggers and configure them to share build results via Pub/Sub
Use a cloudbuild.yaml that checks the branch name and file list in a conditional step and exits non-zero if the conditions are not met
Set a Pub/Sub trigger that subscribes to repository push events and filters by branch and path in the subscription filter
questions.explanation: Cloud Build trigger configuration supports both a branch name pattern (branchName) and an includedFiles glob list. When both are configured, the trigger only fires when a push matches the branch pattern AND at least one changed file matches the includedFiles glob. This is the native, declarative way to implement compound conditions. Using two separate triggers cannot enforce a logical AND on a single build. A cloudbuild.yaml conditional step fires after the trigger has already created a build, wasting resources. A Pub/Sub trigger with subscription filters is more complex and does not natively understand changed file paths.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your Cloud Deploy pipeline deploys a GKE service. After a rollout, you want to automatically roll back if Cloud Monitoring detects that the error rate SLO burn rate exceeds a threshold within 10 minutes of deployment. Which Cloud Deploy feature supports this automated rollback based on metrics?
options: Configure a Cloud Monitoring alerting policy and connect it to a Cloud Deploy Automation resource with the repair rollout (rollback) action triggered on the alert
Set a rollbackOnFailure: true flag in the delivery pipeline stage; Cloud Deploy polls Cloud Monitoring automatically
Create a Cloud Build step in a postdeploy action that queries Cloud Monitoring and calls gcloud deploy rollouts rollback if the threshold is exceeded
Use Cloud Deploy's built-in canary analysis that reads from Cloud Monitoring metrics and rolls back automatically if the canary fails
questions.explanation: Cloud Deploy Automation resources support a repair rollout action that can be triggered by external events including Cloud Monitoring alert policies firing. By connecting a burn-rate alert policy to a Cloud Deploy Automation, you get automated rollback when metric thresholds are breached after a deployment. There is no rollbackOnFailure flag in Cloud Deploy pipeline stage configuration. A Cloud Build postdeploy step that queries metrics and calls rollback is a valid workaround but is more complex than using the native Automation feature. Cloud Deploy's canary analysis (via Skaffold) provides metric-based gating but operates during the rollout progression, not as a post-completion monitor.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: A team is migrating from Jenkins to Cloud Build. Their Jenkins pipeline uses a shared library of reusable Groovy functions imported across many jobs. What is the equivalent pattern in Cloud Build for sharing reusable build logic across multiple pipelines?
options: Cloud Build does not support reusable logic; each cloudbuild.yaml must be self-contained
Store common steps as custom Docker images published to Artifact Registry; reference them by image name in the name field of steps across multiple cloudbuild.yaml files
Use Cloud Build triggers with the includedFiles filter to only run relevant steps per pipeline
Define a Cloud Build worker pool with pre-installed shared libraries that all builds inherit
questions.explanation: In Cloud Build, reusable logic is encapsulated in custom container images (sometimes called Cloud Builders). You build an image that contains your shared scripts, tools, or logic, push it to Artifact Registry, and reference it as the name of a step in any cloudbuild.yaml. This is the idiomatic Cloud Build equivalent of shared Jenkins libraries. includedFiles controls trigger conditions, not step logic sharing. Private worker pools customize machine specs and network access, not the build step code available to pipelines.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: You want to implement GitOps for your GKE workloads: the cluster state should automatically reconcile to match the manifests stored in a Git repository, without Cloud Build triggering explicit kubectl apply commands. Which GCP-native tool enables this continuous reconciliation model?
options: Cloud Deploy with a delivery pipeline that polls the Git repository every minute for changes
Config Sync (part of Config Management on GKE), which continuously watches a Git repository and reconciles the cluster state to match the manifests
Cloud Build with a push trigger that runs kubectl apply on every commit to the manifests repository
Anthos Config Management Constraints Framework, which enforces policy but does not reconcile manifest state
questions.explanation: Config Sync is the GCP-native GitOps engine included in GKE's Config Management add-on. It runs as a controller in the cluster and continuously reconciles the live cluster state against the declared state in a configured Git repository, applying any drift corrections without external pipeline invocations. Cloud Deploy requires explicit release and promotion actions and does not poll Git autonomously. A Cloud Build push trigger applies on commits but does not detect or correct drift between deployments. The Constraints Framework is a policy enforcement tool, not a reconciliation engine.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your Artifact Registry Docker repository stores images for many services. An image for a legacy service has not been pushed or pulled in 18 months and occupies significant storage. You want to automatically clean up images that have not been accessed in over 12 months without manual intervention. Which Artifact Registry feature handles this?
options: Enable versioned object lifecycle management on the Cloud Storage bucket backing Artifact Registry
Configure a cleanup policy on the Artifact Registry repository that deletes untagged or stale images based on age or last-access criteria
Write a Cloud Scheduler job that runs gcloud artifacts docker images list and deletes old images via a Cloud Run function
Set a retention policy on the repository that locks images after 12 months, preventing further writes but not deleting existing images
questions.explanation: Artifact Registry supports repository-level cleanup policies that automatically delete images matching criteria such as tag status (untagged), creation age, or keep-most-recent count. Once configured, the cleanup runs periodically without manual intervention. Artifact Registry does not use Cloud Storage bucket lifecycle rules as its storage is managed internally. A Cloud Scheduler and Cloud Run solution works but adds operational overhead and is not the native approach. Retention policies in Artifact Registry prevent deletion to satisfy compliance requirements -- they are the opposite of cleanup.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your Cloud Build pipeline runs in a private pool peered to a Shared VPC. The build steps need to push images to Artifact Registry and store build logs in Cloud Storage, but all traffic must stay on Google's private network. The private pool has NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS set. What additional VPC configuration is required for the build steps to reach these Google APIs?
options: Add external IP addresses to the private pool workers to allow direct internet access to Google APIs
Configure a Cloud NAT gateway on the VPC to translate private pool worker requests to Google API endpoints
Enable Private Google Access on the subnet used for the private pool VPC peering so that workers can reach Google APIs over internal IP addresses
Deploy a Squid proxy VM in the VPC and configure the build steps to use it as an HTTP proxy for all Google API calls
questions.explanation: When a Cloud Build private pool has NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS set, workers have no direct internet access. To reach Google APIs (Artifact Registry, Cloud Storage, etc.), Private Google Access must be enabled on the peered VPC subnet. Private Google Access allows instances without external IPs to reach Google API endpoints using their internal routing, keeping all traffic on Google's backbone without traversing the internet. Adding external IPs defeats the purpose of NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS. Cloud NAT provides internet access for internal instances but the traffic would still exit Google's private backbone. A proxy VM adds complexity and the proxy itself would need internet access to reach Google APIs.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: A team wants to reduce the time their Cloud Build pipeline spends on Docker image builds. The Dockerfile has 12 layers; the first 8 layers (base OS, system dependencies) change rarely, while the last 4 layers (application code) change on every commit. Which Docker build optimization in Cloud Build is MOST effective?
options: Use FROM scratch as the base image to minimize the number of layers
Use kaniko with --cache=true and --cache-repo pointing to Artifact Registry so unchanged early layers are pulled from cache instead of rebuilt
Increase Cloud Build machine type to n1-highcpu-8 to speed up layer hashing
Use docker build --no-cache to avoid stale cache issues and always build all layers fresh
questions.explanation: Kaniko's remote layer caching pushes each built layer to an OCI registry (Artifact Registry) and on subsequent builds pulls cached layers for instructions that have not changed. Since the first 8 layers are stable, they will be cache hits on every build, dramatically reducing build time. Only the 4 application layers that change every commit need to be rebuilt. FROM scratch eliminates the base image entirely and is only suitable for statically compiled binaries. Increasing machine type speeds up CPU-bound operations but image layer rebuilding is often I/O or network bound. --no-cache disables all caching and would make every build slower.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Choose TWO correct statements about Cloud Deploy delivery pipelines and releases. (Choose TWO)
options: A release is a snapshot of the application manifests and container images at a point in time; it is created once and then promoted through targets without modification
Each promotion to a new target creates a new release with a fresh manifest snapshot
A delivery pipeline defines the ordered sequence of targets through which releases are promoted
Targets in a Cloud Deploy pipeline must all be of the same type (e.g., all GKE or all Cloud Run)
questions.explanation: In Cloud Deploy, a release is an immutable snapshot: once created it captures the manifest source and rendered configuration, and that same release is promoted through each target in the pipeline. This ensures consistency across environments. The delivery pipeline defines the ordered list of stages and their associated targets, governing the promotion path. Promoting to a new target does not create a new release; it creates a new rollout of the same release. Cloud Deploy pipelines support mixed target types (GKE, Cloud Run, Anthos) in the same pipeline.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your team's Cloud Build pipeline builds a Go binary and then packages it into a minimal Docker image. Build times are slow because the Go compilation step re-downloads all modules on every run. The go.sum file rarely changes. What is the MOST effective caching strategy for this workload in Cloud Build?
options: Use a multi-stage Dockerfile where the first stage caches the Go module download layer; Cloud Build reuses Docker layer cache automatically across builds

At the start of the build, restore the GOPATH module cache from a Cloud Storage bucket; at the end, persist it back to Cloud Storage; skip the restore if go.sum has not changed since the last cached run
Set the GOFLAGS=-mod=vendor environment variable and commit the vendor directory to the repository so modules are never downloaded during builds
Use a Cloud Build private pool with a larger disk size so the module cache persists on the worker disk between builds
questions.explanation: The recommended pattern for cross-build Go module caching in Cloud Build is to store the module cache ($GOPATH/pkg/mod) in a Cloud Storage bucket and restore it at build start. By checking whether go.sum has changed since the last cache write (e.g., by comparing a stored hash), you can skip restoration and let Go re-download only when dependencies actually change. Docker layer caching for Go modules only works reliably within kaniko builds using --cache-repo and is not automatic in standard Cloud Build. Vendoring works but commits potentially large binaries to source control. Private pool disk is ephemeral; each build job may run on a different worker, so the module cache is not preserved between builds.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: A Cloud Deploy pipeline deploys a service to a GKE cluster. The Skaffold configuration uses Helm for rendering. During a rollout, you need to override the image tag for the Helm chart without modifying the chart values file. Which Cloud Deploy configuration allows per-release image tag overrides?
options: Set the image tag as a Cloud Build substitution and pass it through to the release via a trigger parameter

Specify the container image in the images block of the Cloud Deploy release creation command (gcloud deploy releases create --images); Cloud Deploy and Skaffold use this to substitute the image reference in the Helm chart
Modify the Helm values.yaml in the Git repository and create a new commit before each release
Use a Cloud Deploy Automation to update the Helm chart before each rollout
questions.explanation: When creating a Cloud Deploy release with gcloud deploy releases create, you can pass --images=IMAGE_NAME=IMAGE_REFERENCE to specify the exact container image (including digest or tag) to use. Skaffold processes this image map and substitutes the matching image references in the Helm chart templates or values. This is the standard way to parameterize image versions in Cloud Deploy without modifying source manifests per release. Cloud Build substitutions operate at build time, before the release exists. Modifying values.yaml for each release creates commit noise and couples the release process to source control changes. Automations handle pipeline flow, not manifest rendering.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your organization requires that all Cloud Build pipelines run with least-privilege service accounts. A pipeline only needs to push images to one specific Artifact Registry repository and write logs to Cloud Logging. Which TWO IAM roles should be granted to the Cloud Build service account?
options: roles/artifactregistry.writer on the specific Artifact Registry repository
roles/artifactregistry.admin on the project to ensure the service account can manage the repository
roles/logging.logWriter on the project to allow writing build logs to Cloud Logging
roles/editor on the project to ensure broad access for any build operation
questions.explanation: Least-privilege means granting only the permissions needed. roles/artifactregistry.writer scoped to the specific repository grants push access without admin privileges over the registry. roles/logging.logWriter allows the service account to write log entries to Cloud Logging without broader project permissions. roles/artifactregistry.admin grants permissions to create, delete, and manage repositories -- excessive for a pipeline that only pushes images. roles/editor grants broad write access across the entire project, violating least-privilege.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: A webhook trigger is configured in Cloud Build to receive events from an external system. The external system sends a JSON payload. You want to extract the value of the field data.version from the payload and use it as the image tag in the build. Which Cloud Build feature allows this?
options: Webhook triggers automatically parse JSON payloads and expose all fields as substitution variables
Use a CEL (Common Expression Language) filter expression in the trigger to bind data.version to a user-defined substitution such as $_VERSION, then reference $_VERSION in the cloudbuild.yaml
Write a Cloud Functions function that receives the webhook, parses the JSON, and invokes the Cloud Build API with the version as a substitution
Base64-decode the webhook payload in the first Cloud Build step and extract the version using jq
questions.explanation: Cloud Build webhook triggers support CEL expressions for both filtering and payload binding. You can write a substitution binding such as _VERSION: body.data.version to extract a field from the incoming JSON payload and map it to a user-defined substitution. This substitution is then available as $_VERSION in the cloudbuild.yaml steps. Webhook triggers do not automatically expose all payload fields as substitutions. A Cloud Functions intermediary works but adds operational overhead for something natively supported. Decoding and parsing in a build step happens after the build starts and cannot affect the initial substitution values.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your team runs GKE workloads across three clusters: one each for dev, staging, and production. You want a single Cloud Deploy delivery pipeline to manage releases across all three clusters. How should the targets be configured?
options: Create one target that references all three GKE clusters using a comma-separated cluster list in the gke.cluster field
Create three separate targets, each referencing one GKE cluster by its project, location, and cluster name; reference them as sequential stages in the delivery pipeline
Create three delivery pipelines, one per environment, and link them using Cloud Deploy pipeline chaining
Create a single multi-target that groups all three clusters; all three will be deployed simultaneously on each release
questions.explanation: In Cloud Deploy, each target represents one deployment destination. For three clusters, you define three targets each with a gke block specifying the project, location, and cluster. These targets are then referenced as sequential stages in the delivery pipeline's stages list, enforcing dev -> staging -> production promotion order. A single target cannot reference multiple clusters; the gke.cluster field accepts one cluster. Three separate pipelines lose the unified promotion model and approval gates. A multi-target deploys all clusters simultaneously, which is appropriate for multi-region parallel deploys but not for ordered environment promotion.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your Cloud Build pipeline is responsible for deploying Terraform changes. The pipeline must ensure that even if two builds are triggered simultaneously (e.g., two engineers push at the same time), only one Terraform apply runs at a time to prevent state corruption. Which Cloud Build feature addresses this?
options: Set the build timeout to a very long value so the second build waits for the first to complete
Configure a Cloud Build approval gate on the apply trigger so a human must approve each apply, naturally serializing them
Use a Cloud Build trigger with concurrentBuilds: false (or set the queue depth to 1 and use build queuing) so that concurrent builds for the same trigger are serialized
Store a lock file in Cloud Storage and have the build step check for and acquire the lock before running terraform apply
questions.explanation: Cloud Build supports controlling build concurrency at the trigger level. Setting the trigger to serialize builds (via concurrency controls or a build queue with depth 1) ensures that even if two pushes arrive simultaneously, only one build runs the apply step at a time while the other waits in queue. This is cleaner than application-level lock files, which require careful cleanup logic on build failure. A long timeout does not prevent concurrent execution. Human approval serializes naturally but adds friction and is not fully automated.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: You are designing a CI/CD pipeline for a microservices application with 15 services in a monorepo. You want each service to be built and deployed independently, only when its source files change. Which Cloud Build trigger configuration achieves this efficiently?
options: Create one trigger with a single cloudbuild.yaml that builds all 15 services in parallel on every push
Create 15 separate triggers, one per service, each with an includedFiles filter scoped to that service's directory and its own cloudbuild.yaml at the service root
Create one trigger that runs a shell script to detect changed services and dynamically generates a cloudbuild.yaml for only the changed services
Create a nightly trigger that builds and deploys all 15 services on a schedule regardless of changes
questions.explanation: The idiomatic Cloud Build approach for monorepo CI is a trigger per service, each with an includedFiles filter pointing to that service's directory subtree. When a push changes only service A's files, only service A's trigger fires. Services B through O are unaffected and their pipelines do not run. This provides precise, low-latency CI without cross-service coupling. Building all 15 services on every push wastes build minutes and slows feedback for changes to individual services. Dynamically generating cloudbuild.yaml is fragile and complex. A nightly schedule provides no per-commit validation.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your Cloud Build pipeline builds a container image and pushes it to Artifact Registry. Immediately after the push, a downstream system needs to be notified with the image's full URI including digest. Which Cloud Build mechanism provides this information post-build?
options: Cloud Build writes a build manifest JSON to the build's Cloud Storage logs bucket containing the pushed image URIs and their digests; downstream systems can read this file
The image URI and digest are automatically published to a Pub/Sub topic named cloudbuild-results after every build
Query the Artifact Registry API after the build to find the most recently pushed image in the repository
Parse the Cloud Build build log for the digest line printed by docker push
questions.explanation: When images are declared in the images field of cloudbuild.yaml, Cloud Build pushes them and writes a build result manifest (artifacts.json) to the build's Cloud Storage logs bucket. This JSON file contains the fully qualified image URIs including SHA256 digests for each pushed image. Downstream systems can reliably read this file to get exact image references for deployment. There is no automatic cloudbuild-results Pub/Sub topic for image digests (there is a cloud-builds topic for build state events, but not image digests). Querying the registry for the most recent push is racy in concurrent environments. Parsing log output is brittle.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: A team wants to implement a deployment pipeline for an App Engine Flex service that: (1) deploys a new version, (2) serves 0% of traffic initially, (3) runs automated smoke tests against the new version URL, (4) migrates all traffic to the new version only if smoke tests pass. Which sequence of gcloud commands correctly implements steps 2 through 4?
options: gcloud app deploy --no-promote; run smoke tests against the version URL; if tests pass, gcloud app services set-traffic default --splits NEW_VERSION=1
gcloud app deploy --promote=false; run smoke tests against the default URL; if tests pass, gcloud app deploy again with --promote
gcloud app deploy; run smoke tests against the new version URL; gcloud app versions migrate NEW_VERSION
gcloud app deploy --no-promote; run smoke tests against the default service URL; if tests pass, gcloud app versions start NEW_VERSION
questions.explanation: gcloud app deploy --no-promote deploys the new version to App Engine Flex without sending it any traffic, keeping 100% on the current serving version. Each deployed version has its own URL (https://VERSION_ID-dot-SERVICE-dot-PROJECT.appspot.com) which can be used for smoke testing without affecting user traffic. If tests pass, gcloud app services set-traffic assigns the new version 100% of traffic. Option B's --promote=false is not a valid flag. Option C deploys with promotion (traffic shifts immediately) before tests run. Option D's gcloud app versions start starts a stopped version but does not control traffic splitting.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your organization uses Cloud Deploy across 10 GCP projects. You want to ensure that all delivery pipelines across all projects follow the same naming convention and require approval for production targets. Which approach enforces this at the organization level with the least ongoing maintenance?
options: Send a policy document to all project owners and ask them to comply voluntarily
Use an Organization Policy custom constraint that validates Cloud Deploy pipeline and target configurations, enforcing naming patterns and requireApproval on production targets
Create a centralized Cloud Deploy pipeline in a shared services project and require all teams to use it for production deployments
Audit all Cloud Deploy pipelines weekly using a Cloud Asset Inventory query and file tickets for non-compliant configurations
questions.explanation: Organization Policy Service supports custom constraints that can evaluate the properties of Cloud Deploy resources (delivery pipelines and targets) at create or update time, enforcing naming conventions and mandatory field values like requireApproval: true on production targets. Once applied at the organization or folder level, the constraint blocks non-compliant resource creation automatically with no per-project configuration needed. Voluntary compliance and weekly audits are detective controls, not preventive. A centralized shared pipeline creates a bottleneck and does not prevent teams from creating their own non-compliant pipelines in their projects.
questions.category: Building and implementing CI/CD pipelines for a service

questions.question: Your team wants to create an alerting policy in Cloud Monitoring that fires when the 99th-percentile latency of a Cloud Run service exceeds 800ms for more than 5 consecutive minutes. Which alerting condition type should you use?
options: Uptime check alerting condition that pings the Cloud Run URL every minute
Metric threshold condition on the run.googleapis.com/request_latencies metric using the 99th percentile aligner, with a threshold of 800ms and a duration of 5 minutes
Log-based alert triggered when Cloud Logging receives a log entry with latency_ms > 800
Budget alert threshold set on the Cloud Run service cost
questions.explanation: Cloud Monitoring metric threshold conditions evaluate an aggregated time series against a threshold.
